title: The Macro Minute:  What did the delayed PCE Report signal about US consumer health and Fed policy?

Happy Friday out there, team 42. It's
your skier here at Darius Del to present
our macro minute for Friday, December
5th, 2025. It's the first Friday of the
month. We might not have a jobs report,
but we got a PC report. So, you know
what? It's time for the macro scouting
report. We'll be publishing our December
2025 macro scouting report to our global
investor community this afternoon. Very
pumped to present that material. So, uh
let's get into our executive summary. Um
this today's key macro questions are uh
what did the delayed September PC report
signal about the health of the US
consumer and what did it signal about
the Fed's current policy setting. The
short answers are the delayed September
PC report supported our resilient US
economy theme. The strong negative
impulses in the core and housing PC
deflators are the latest of many data
points that suggest the Fed is being
obtuse regarding the level of the policy
rate. Terms of the analytical nuance,
the September PC report supported our
resilient US economy theme via the weak
positive impulses and real headline PCE
and real goods PCE as well as the strong
positive impulse in real services PC.
The September PC report supported our
Brazilian US economy theme via the weak
positive impulses in nominal and real
disposable personal income and the
persistent below trend personal savings
rate which is an indication of the West
Village Montto effect. If you don't know
what that is, Google it. It's probably
online somewhere. The September PC
report supported our resilient US
economy theme via the weak positive
impulse in nominal employee compensation
which more than offset a strong negative
impulse in government transfer payments.
The September PC report supported our
resilient US economy theme via the weak
positive impulse in nominal wage and
salary compensation which mirrored the
weak positive impulse in our private
sector labor income metric, a key
leading indicator for the health of the
US consumer. Finally, the weak positive
impulses in headline and super core PC
deflators supported our tariff pig in
the inflation python thesis. The Fed's
own research suggests tariff shocks are
disinflationary negative shocks to
aggregate demand. FYI, the bond market
is not especially concerned about sticky
inflation and thinks the Fed funds rate
should be at or below 3%. As always,
members of our global investor community
can trust that our institutional grade
expansion overlays, Kiss and Dr. MO will
help our portfolios navigate these
emergent risks better than investors
attempting to manage risk without
systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone. Transition
to our 42 macro dashboard. As always, we
wrap up the question from our community.
Uh this was not a question, but I
thought it was very important to
highlight uh in the context of what
we've been calling out as the number one
risk to the 42 macro paradigm C bull
market that we got started for you in
April. I hope you've been participating
since April. So, if you haven't been
participating in making a ton of money
since April, that's okay. There's plenty
of money to make in the future. Just
join us and participate in our case of
Dr. Mo Signals. Um, but getting back
into uh this, we've been calling out for
months now that the number one risk to
this this bull market that we got
started for you back in April is a tardy
pile Fed that falls so far behind the
rate and liquidity curves that it
ultimately causes a meaningful
contraction in the labor market and more
importantly for asset markets a
meaningful contraction in the repo
market in terms of a a funding crisis.
We saw that outcome occur uh the latter
outcome occur uh in 2018 and 2019 and
obviously we saw a crash uh in the S&P
uh and risk assets uh Q418 as a function
of that of that uh policy gap one of a

unpack uh in in today's uh macro
scouting report. Not to be pjorative

turning. You basically have to the core
question of our time right now is do you

society or do you destroy the bond
market? I don't know if I have the

hard question to answer. There are no
right answers by the way and Paul said
that as much said as much rather um back
in October. Uh so uh getting to uh the
today's uh comment here in our in our in
our global investor community. This
one's titled sulfur fed funds spreads
says reserves may arrive sooner than
expected. The market has priced out
delayed liquidity injections from the
Fed with an earlier balance sheet uh
expansion baked into markets. Trading
desks now expect the Fed's reserve
management operation purchases where
buys bills and with newly minted
reserves to maintain Apple liquidity to
begin closer to January. um you know if
that's true that will be awesome and
that would you know kind of a
millilarate what we again see is the key
risk to the 42 macro paradigm cable
market which is again the Fed falling
further behind the rate and liquidity
curves. So taking to my Bloomberg here
where we show a chart of repo market
liquidity or gauges of repo market
liquidity the top panel shows um the red
line in both panels actually is the
secured overnight financing rate. The
red line in the top panel is the
interest on reserve balances and the red
line in the bottom panel is uh the
factor Fed's funds rate. And the reason
we track the spread between these two uh
rather rather these three rates uh uh
the top panel uh the spread um between
sulfur and the interest on reserve
balances essentially shows an indication
of how uh scarce or abundant liquidity
is in the repo market. For example, uh
whenever you have a positive spread with
sofur relative to the IRB, it suggests
that there's not enough reserves in the
system to essentially beat that rate
down below IRB which is supposed to be
because banks aren't going to lend below
IRB. So essentially tells you that the
GSC's and the money market funds are
running out of cash. Um and that's um
that's a dynamic that is um perpetu
that's currently being perpetuated by
this above trend level of the TGA
balance which by the way kind of has to
stay that way given the years of of
dovish financing policy that we've uh
accumulated uh with Treasury Secretary
Yellen and Treasury Secretary uh Scott
Bessant. uh transition and looking at
the bottom panel here. Uh so the bottom
panel shows the sulfur rate again in the
blue. Uh the red line and the bottom
panel is the effective funds rate. Uh
it's kind of a dead market but a similar
dynamic. Uh typically what you see is
when you have these spikes uh in the
sulfur effective funds rate market. Uh
one's a secured lending market. Sulfur
one's an unsecured lending market. So
typically when you see that that spread
spike, it's a suggestion that there's
either collateral shortage or again some
sort of shortage of liquidity or both.
And so, as you can see, we've been
accumulating funding stress, you know,
really in in and so for effective funds
back since midsummer. And then we've
been accumulating funding stress um uh
in in so far IRB since the early fall.
Uh and then, you know, obviously we're
starting to see, you know, much higher
trending levels of the spread in terms
of the positive spread. So, that was a
real clear warning sign that, hey, we
are definitely transitioning from a
level of ample reserves to something
that's slightly below that. And
eventually [clears throat] after you go
forward enough in time, you're going to
go from ample reserves to something
slightly below that to not enough
reserves. And that's when you go poof uh
markets crash. And so obviously we saw
uh the peripheral vaso constriction that
we observed in repo in in the in the in
the crypto markets and in the
commodities markets in recent months was
in our opinion a function of this
because again the further you're away
from the curl of the financial system,
